text: This meeting is being recorded.
Going good, going good.
How are you?
How's the day been?
Yeah, I'm doing great.
It gets so busy right before the holidays, but I'm hanging in there.
I can see light at the end of the tunnel.
How about yourself?
Very, very similar.
Getting things wrapped up as people head out for holidays and out of offices are going to be more common in a few days.
So yeah, getting things done before the holidays.
Nice.
Do you have any plans for the holidays?
Traveling anywhere?
Not really.
I just did like an extended one and a half month travel in October to November.
So I guess, yeah, I'm going to take it easy.
Stay home, stay indoors because it's cold.
Makes total sense.
Where were you traveling for so long?
That's a long trip.
Yeah, indeed, indeed.
So I started off in New York for a conference where we were hosting the conference and then went to Prague for a week.
Then went to Greece for a company, All Hands for a week, and then went to India for four weeks.
And on the way back, stopped in London for a week, and then home.
Man, that is incredible.
And I was looking at your background a little bit, and you're no stranger to traveling that frequently.
know, you've like moved across the world, and I'm sure you have a lot of stamps on your passport.
I do, I do.
I love that.
Yes, I enjoy traveling a lot.
I mean, be it for work, be it personal, pleasure, anything.
So, yeah, that's a no problem.
Yeah, I love traveling as well.
Definitely need to do more of it.
I'm based in Houston, and I lived in San Francisco in New York for a couple years.
But my fiance and I were probably rooted in Texas, just being close to our families and having our wedding coming up in April.
Oh, nice.
Congratulations.
Yeah.
How's the prep coming along?
Pretty good.
Yeah, the last couple of vendors, you know?
Yeah.
Like you learn about vendors you didn't even know existed.
know, she mentioned like a vendor, a vendor for linens.
I'm like, what are linens?
Why do they cost so much?
But we're very close.
We're at the finish line here.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Sounds like fun.
Definitely.
Well, it's great to meet you and super excited to get to know you better.
For context, I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX and we're a big sales team of one.
So naturally, we need some more help.
And I'm just really excited because, you know, this person that we're going to bring on, we're definitely going to collaborate more than account executives typically do.
There's just like so much process that has been put in motion that we need to turn into, right, like a final product.
There's so much need to collaborate cross-functionally and with your background, like doing both sales and post-sales.
I'm just excited.
It's.

Like learn more about some of the work that you've done in both areas and answer any questions that you have about, you know, the way I've been working here at GrowthX and, you know, what we're focused on scaling next year.

So I guess to kick it off, you know, typical start to the interview, would just love to like hear about your journey going from sales to customer success and why you would be interested in like moving back to sales.
We'll dig deeper into some of your experience, but just help me to get to know your story a little bit better and the experience you've had on both sides.
Yeah, absolutely.
Yeah.
First of all, thanks for taking this time.
I know it's a busy season, getting contracts out, everything.
So really appreciate you taking this time.
Yeah, for talking about my experience, I have been in both sides of the world, like pre-sales to sales to post-sales to customers.

I think fairly equally, like I have a well-balanced experience throughout and I'm very fortunate that it has turned out this way because I understand both sides of the world, like before buying, what do prospects think, how are they evaluating solutions, what's their journey like, and then what happens after buying, like what do they expect from their vendors and things.
So I really enjoy both sides of the world.
And yeah, right now I'm in kind of in between of both, like AE to customer success.
I'm an account manager in Sanity.

A big chunk of what I do overlaps with account executives responsibilities, which is like discovery calls, understanding pain points, reaching out to the wider team, establishing, multi-threading across different business units in an organization, going through contracts, and still upselling and cross-selling.
So a large amount of what I do now definitely overlaps with an AE's role.
So I was like, I might as well go back to being an AE then if I'm doing pretty much what an AE does.
So which is why I started looking out and then I've been following GrowthX since I think July.
That's when July or August, that's when Marcel added me on LinkedIn.
And I started following GrowthX's journey and I was like, okay, this looks really, really amazing for two reasons.

Number one, I've been in the content space for quite a while now and with AI coming into play more and more evidently and it's becoming a crowded space, it's becoming more and more evident that companies, brands, SaaS companies, everyone have to think about content very strategically.
And right now, Sanity is a content management space itself and also I bring in AI experience where in my previous company, I was...
Building bespoke workflows for senior living and healthcare companies across the states.
So basically, I was implementing and deploying chatbots on their websites.
So I used to do a lot of research on those companies, understand their customers, what is their tone and messaging, and I would design workflows and deploy those chatbots on the websites.
So those were the two key reasons, which is why GrowthX has been on my mind ever since I bumped into it back in July.
And I've been following your journey from then on, like from 7 million to 11 million to your podcast yesterday, which was amazing.
So yeah, that's my background and my interest.
Wow, I love that.
And bonus points for recognizing the podcast.
Of course, how could I not?
That's the first business podcast I've been a part of, and hopefully I can do more.
You know, Marcel does such a great job of building in public.
He is so natural in front of a camera.
I mean, I watched his behind the scenes with Lovable.
Oh, my God.
He's so comfortable.
It's incredible, too, how he can just immediately, like, lock in with very little prep, be completely in the moment, and just, like, trust his expertise, right?
And week two, when I joined, I got to go to San Francisco, and he was running sales since there wasn't a salesperson.
So I basically got to just follow him around, be in every meeting he was a part of.
And that was, like, the best training, just to, like, learn by example, right?
Absolutely, And then absorb, like, you know what?
Sometimes it is hard to context switch in sales, but Marcel's the CEO of this business, and he's doing it in addition to all these other things.
So sometimes you do just have to, like, trust what you know and not be in your head so much, as I'm sure you've experienced, you know, being on the sales and the post-sales side.
me back to Storyblock.
That kind of seems like the first, like, true eight.

The experience that you had, and maybe you did sales before that, but I'd love to just learn more about some of the, like, I think you mentioned, like, a playbook that you built or some of the foundational processes.
Like, just walk me through that experience a bit more and touch on some of the process that you built as an account executive there.
For sure.
Yeah.
Similar to your journey with GrowthX, I was the first sales hire.
I was the first account executive at Storyblock.
So, again, learning from the big people, from the bosses is the best way to learn.
So, I had that privilege at Storyblock being the first hire.
So, yeah, I was selling to pretty much all over the world because there was no other salesperson there.
So, some of the key logos that I sold to were T-Mobile, TomTom, Walmart, Netflix.
So, I did sell to, like, companies like this.
And Storyblock had never had a six-figure deal up until I sold to T-Mobile.
That was the first six.
That's good deal that StoryBlock had.
I always talk about that because that was my intense four-month-long RFP process, very extensive, like 30-page RFP, tons and tons of questions about security, IT, legal, and so on.

So that was a great learning experience for me for various reasons, like how elaborate these processes are, what do companies expect from vendors like StoryBlock and many others, what are the standards that they're expecting, given that it's T-Mobile, it's one of the probably, I don't know, Fortune 500, Fortune 1000 companies in the world, and how to navigate all of that within a small ecosystem inside of StoryBlock.
Where do I get all that information from?
I didn't know that because there was no big team of like security operations people to answer responses in a spreadsheet.
So I had to navigate with that one.
Documents saying, I have done as much as I could with the knowledge that I have.
I need your help from engineering.
I need your help from security.
I need your help from finance and legal about company registration and things like that.
So I learned a lot about navigating within Storyblock to different departments and like collaboratively working and putting together that RFP.
So that's when I realized, okay, we need a playbook on how to navigate this.
So I started building a playbook.
I started building a library, a knowledge base of where we can find information about security, about a data center, about our story blocks, registration and license and insurance certificates.
So that's one part where I sort of built a playbook.
And then the other part was the navigating sales process itself.
Like what happens once you book a discovery call?
How should the discovery call go?
What are the questions that we should ask?
What is the information we need?
What happens after discovery call?
Who should the demo be with?
How long should the demo be with?
Are there particular topics or items that the prospect needs or is interested to see in the demo?
If yes, I convey that beforehand to the solutions engineers saying, hey, they're interested in specific topics, let's invest more time here.
And then after the demo, again, follow up saying we discussed X, Y, Z.
Here are some additional resources and things.
And afterwards, we either have another deep dive or we do a financial feasibility like, hey, this is what it costs.
This is how our pricing is structured.
Does this still align with what you're looking for?
And then what follows afterwards is perhaps there is an involvement from procurement, our dear friends in the sales world.
We kind of have love-hate relationship with them.
So, yeah.
know how that goes.
Yep.
Exactly.
Yeah.
So, yeah, navigate through procurement, which often is a lot of...
Back and forth with their legal, our legal, and then there's IT involved.
And yeah, all along, I strongly believe that multi-threading is important.
I need to have stakeholders that know me by name across various departments.
That's how I will be able to practice in this much time.
So yeah, I kind of defined that playbook of sales processes and just navigating the whole deal from start to finish and then what happens afterwards.
Yeah, great, great answer.

And, you know, I can tell that like you were very involved in that process because it comes off so naturally, you know, when you talk about it because it's a lot of hard work and a lot of moving things forward day by day.
This is just a curiosity question.
No wrong or right answer.
What made you go from there to customer success?
Like what made you switch out of sales into customer success as your next step?
For sure.
Yeah.
So going to customer success was not.

So I hadn't decided and I didn't have my mind that I will go to CSM, but like every other person in this day and age or probably a few years ago, when there is AI on the topic or if there is an AI opportunity, like I wanted to jump on it.
I wanted to take it because it was like such a hot topic.

It was still a hot topic, but at the time it was like, yeah, you'll get to build chatbots, you'll get to do prompt engineering, you'll get to like deploy this in customer's website where it's going to answer questions that the users are going to ask.
So that seemed very fascinating to me because it was AI at play and it was a curious cat in me that said, okay, yes, it's AI, although it's CS, I will take it.
Yeah.
And technically it was not a quintessential CS role, I would say, because it was more like an implementation integration kind of a role.
For sure.
Makes perfect sense.
Thanks for helping me understand that.
Of course.
So thinking about, you know, sales, either at Sanity or Storyblock, tell me about how you would position the product's value.
You could choose one of the two.
doesn't have to be both.
But like, how would you position the product's value to like a CMO or a go-to-market leader in a deal or in a, you know, client relationship?
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.

So what is of importance these days for the C-levels is that the velocity of content, like how quickly are his teams, her teams able to generate content and making the most of being able to deliver and time to market?
Because we have to run fast.
We have to move fast.
And with AI, again, it's possible.
Not that we have to sit for hours or hire like content people, generate variations of it and get it approved and stuff.
No, we don't have to do that.
With AI, it's fast and it's possible.
So I would understand what the CMO's objectives are when it comes to content.
If they are treating content like data, like this is not a one-time thing, like I'll update my website today and forget about it.
No, that's not the way.
Content is a living thing.
It has to be re-treated, refined, re-tuned.
And I would show that with Sanity, it is so flexible.
And also there is AI integrated within Sanity.
So you can re-tune, re-fine, make changes on the fly, also preview it while you're doing those changes.
And that's how quickly you can publish pages.

And although Sanity is a developer-focused portal, we have enough content strategists who can spend time and coach your authors and editors within the portal to enable them that they can be independent and they don't have to rely on developers.
So I would show the value of how quickly it is possible to do these things and what that means in terms of time saved.
In terms of resources allocated, saved, in terms of, of course, cost efficiency.
So I think these are three things that would matter for a C-level.
So I would approach it from that perspective.
Makes sense.
With the clients that you'd be working with, were there often strong marketing champions?
Or would you often have to build those since the developers are the ones who are typically singing Stanity's praises initially?
It's a combination of both, although I would say it's 60-65% of engineering folks that we engage with more commonly.
And then there's like 40-35% of marketing, digital merchandising, content authoring, those sort of stakeholders.
So yeah, it's more of the technical and engineering audience.
What would be the hardest objection to overcome from the marketing audience?
Since, you know, the surface level, they might feel like this is built for development.
Developers, not my team.
Tell me how you would overcome that and build fans out of the marketing side.
For sure.
Yeah, that's in fact the most common one that we hear because Sanity definitely comes across a very like a developer focused and technical tool.

For that, like what I have done is I have like demo loops from my colleagues where they are like doing a quick walkthrough of how a content author is kind of like changing a text in the header line of a product, for example.
So it's like a quick 10 second demo loop.
I show them, I can show you a quick demo loop of how a content manager, for example, in Nordstrom is editing this product page in like less than 20 seconds.
And that has been very helpful for me.
So to show them how it is done so quickly gives them a lot of confidence.
And then I have also offered that let's do a workshop together.
Let's let's spend some hands on keyboard time together.
And then I have facilitated some of those.
Which is also appreciated and taken very well.
So, yeah, that's my approach.
No better way than to help the client or prospect see what using your product or service would be like in their world.

That's one thing we're working on really hard here is how can we start to go from demoing workflows in certain clients' workspaces to having a test workspace where we can actually create artifacts for them.
Right.
Or create a proof of concept, piece of content so that they can actually start to envision, okay, well, here's what GrowthX could do for me specifically.

You mentioned, and this was really good, you know, you mentioned the time savings, the cost efficiency, resource allocation, any other like metrics or outcomes you would use to connect sanity or story block to the client's growth strategy?
Uh, well, since content is a big part of what.

What Sanity is doing for our customers, I would definitely try to understand on other metrics like how much content have you published, like how many pieces of articles or blogs, or how much, what's the velocity you're tracking in comparison to how you did it before Sanity.
So if we are tracking those, I think those are also very handy.
And also, how are those blogs performing?
Like you publish this this week, you publish another blog, like two weeks from now, how are the two doing?
Is the timing correct?
Is the interval between the two correct?
Or do you have to do more?
If you have to do more, how can you do that?
So those are some of the metrics that I would also additionally like to capture, like how much content was published?
What was published?
When was it published?
What are the intervals between which they published and stuff?
It makes sense.
And I guess what's the outcome that they're hoping to achieve by creating more content at the end of the day?
I think they are, again, it depends who the customer is.
If my customer is Nordstrom, I'm assuming they want definitely more traffic on their side, more traffic meaning more conversions.
So that's what they're hoping to see.
And if it's for, for example, loyalty members, they definitely want those people to be signed in.
And with that signed in, Nordstrom can actually use their customer journey and come up with more and more personalization for those loyalty members and stuff.
So I think end of the day, conversion and traffic is what matters.
Perfect.
That's what I was looking for.
Thank you.
Thanks for proving, Tyler.
I appreciate it.
No, no, no, no.
Great, great job.
Have you mainly worked with e-commerce and retail?
Have you had any more like B2B software companies that you've had as clients or customers that you've sold to?
No worries.
I'm just asking because we do sell.
So a lot of B2B software companies here and getting a sense that if you've had clients or sold deals like that before.
So sure.
I have a mixed bag of customers.
I have software companies.
I have retail and e-commerce.
I have travel companies.
I can even give you examples like Braze, Brex.
These are some of the companies that I work with from like the SaaS world, from retail world, Nordstrom, like I mentioned, Arc'teryx.
And from hospitality world, Expedia is my customer.
Marriott is my customer.
And yeah, social media, Pinterest is my customer.
I'm just looking at my customer list here.
These are great.
No, incredible, incredible variety and logos.
Exactly.
I have a mixed bag.
We just brought on Brex as a customer at GrowthX, which is exciting.
Congratulations.
Yeah, yeah.
Hopefully, Hopefully, yeah.
So let's say you're working at GrowthX, maybe you've got some good relationships at that account.
Absolutely, 100%, yes, yes.
Okay, let's think about, we don't need to do this as much at GrowthX, outbound.
We have the privilege and the gift of having all of our deals pretty much come inbound from Marcel's incredible network, the building in public that we do through our workshops and community.

But what's an example of like an outreach experiment or a campaign that you ran that, you know, either consistently booked meetings when you were an AE or consistently got responses if we wanted to think about more of the CS world?
Yeah, for sure.
So something that has worked for me is the, I don't believe in like 21 step, 15 step kind of cadences.
No, no one has time for following through that many, nor do we get any sort of engagement and responses with that kind of cadence.
So I believe in like short, like seven step, eight
It steps out of cadences.
And what has worked for me traditionally or historically when I was in StoryBlock as well as in Sanity is a crisp one-line question as to how are you achieving X today?

And then a crisp one-line response with a customer story either in their industry or something relevant to how they operate, saying Sanity works with company Y in this industry and has helped achieve content velocity of 42% more in comparison to what it was in June of 2024.
Would you be curious to know more on how they did this?

So I believe in crisp messaging and definitely for Hook, I believe in personalization, which comes from a lot of research on their LinkedIn or some blog posts that they would have written or any other platforms.
So for Hook, I believe in personalization and for messaging.
It has to be a one-line problem statement that they might have and a one-line problem statement that my product would have solved for a customer in similar industry.
I love that approach.
Attention is hard to come by these days, and you got to get to the point.
Exactly, yeah, yeah.
Okay, last bit here, and then would love to turn it over to you to ask me any questions that are on your mind.
And it could be, this sort of line of questioning, you know, it could be about the T-Mobile deal.
It could be about a different deal.
Totally up to you.

But I just want to go deeper into, like, the story of how you actually closed, you know, one of your bigger deals to help me sort of understand the different steps in the process and what, you know, made the work that you did, right, personally impactful into making sure the deal was won.
Mm-hmm, yeah, absolutely.
Believe in building relationships across multiple departments, like I mentioned before.
And genuinely, I feel the feeling that I get as customers really enjoy working with me and engaging with me.
So I have to have at least three or four contacts across different departments.
And from my key champion, I need to understand who are the relevant stakeholders for this project?
Who should be part of this coming up discovery session, for example?
Who are the absolute stakeholders?
And then who are the basically influencers or even economic buyers?
And most importantly, who holds the budget?
So I want to make sure I have one participant from across these, like kind of metric approach.
Like I have all those participants in that call.
And then I kind of have my ground covered.
Like, okay, this is important for this person.
And this person holds the budget.
So I have information.
information.
around how to structure this deal going forward.
And then I know I have to build relationship with this person because this person is influential.
I have to build relationship with this person because this person holds the budget.
So that's how I approach.
And then I have the discovery call.
after the discovery call, I always believe in booking follow-up calls right when I have people with me in front of me in Zoom.
Because that's the best time that you can discuss your next steps as opposed to going on email, going back and forth.
Does this day, this time work for you?
No, it doesn't.
What else?
So you lose a lot of time in that back and forth.
So I always at least try my best to book my next call while I have them in my current call.
So after the discovery, I book maybe next one is like a technical deep dive or a demo.
And then who are the stakeholders?
Who are the audiences that need to be part of that call?
And do we need 45 minutes?
Do we need one hour?
So much
Whatever that is.
And then I would like to schedule that.
And following that, I would generally like to have a question or a feedback session about the technical feasibility.
Again, that varies from product to product, what is on the offering.
Um, after that, when, when, when we get a go ahead from there, then I would like to involve like, okay, what are the next steps should we look at?
Like commercial aspect of it.
Uh, then I start scoping.
Like this project means these many people, these many user seats.
This is the license looking like.
Then I start scoping and putting a proposal together.
Then I start inviting people who should be part of that call where I walk you through the proposal.
So this is what your license and titles, this is what you will be contracted for and things like that.
Um, and then, yeah, it's, it's the usual procurement step.
They come into play.
They will do a lot of redlining in the contracts, try through hundreds of things that they will not be happy with.
And so sorry to interrupt here.
And this is great.

But but connect this to like a specific deal, like one of the bigger deals that you close, like tell me the story, right, of like, those stages in the process for that deal in particular, as opposed to like more broadly with your process, but makes complete sense.
Yeah, for sure.
Yeah, I can definitely talk about T-Mobile or even something more recently at Sanity as well.
I'll actually take the Sanity instance, because that was a different business unit I brought in into Sanity in my Q2.
So yeah, I was chatting with my champion who currently use Sanity.
And I was generally asking them, it was, I think, after a business review that I did.
I said, I so now that there is a success story, I mean, where else in your organization do you think we can we can take Sanity to?
Are there other teams that are working on content and things like that?
So I started doing basic discovery.
With my champion.
And that's how I uncovered that there is this different business unit based out of Australia that could potentially use Sanity.
So I started inquiring more about like, what do they have right now?
What pain points could they potentially have?
What system do they have?
Do they have a CMS at all?
Or are they in-house built?
Things like that.
So my champion said, I don't know much about it, but I can loop you into someone else.
And I had done some research in the meantime.
So I reached out to my champion and asked, is this the person that we are talking about?
So I had looked up on LinkedIn who that team could potentially be.
And I asked my champion and the champion said, yeah, that's the team that I was talking about.
So we kind of got intros.
And yeah, I had my first discovery call where I said, this is Sanity.
This is what we do here.
Your other business unit uses Sanity for this use.
I'm keen to know how we can help you in accelerating content across your business unit as well.

So did some deep discovery and then I involved a solutions engineer to do a technical discovery saying what front-end framework do you have, where do you host your data, and basically all things that gets connected together.
So we did a technical discovery and after that, what did we do?
So yeah, we did another follow-up session, which was more like a working session, like they wanted to build a proof of concept.
So we did like another 45-minute kind of hands-on keyboard kind of working session in their environment.
And again, that was also scoping for more discovery and understanding what pain points they had and how they do things today versus what is potentially possible with Sanity.
So we did that and then after that, I went.
So back to my original stakeholder and champion saying, hey, we did this POC and it seems like there is a fit here.
There is a solution alignment here.
For next steps, I'm thinking about this.
Should we talk about like scoping on what the license looks like and how many users we are looking at, what data sets or what bandwidth?
I mean, those are SKUs here in Sanity.
What are we looking at?
And so then I organized a scoping call and we went through how their organization is structured and who all would need access to sanity and how many environments they would need and things.
So based off of that, I can structure a proposal.
So I took that back and structured a proposal to them and then walked them through that.
This is your enterprise tier plan.
You get this SLA in this, but if you need more, you will have to be on this tier and so on and so forth.
So this is what your license looks like.
This is your support channel.
You can throw in all your questions in Teams or Slack or whatever.
So I did walk them through the proposal.
And then, of course, there was some negotiation on the dollar amount and things.
And salespeople doing the sales trick saying, yeah, if you can sign by this day and blah, blah, blah, we'll do this for you and things.
Yeah.
And then there was a procurement.
That procurement was tricky.
They were challenging, as many of them.
We did go back and forth with a few language, verbiage items in the contract.
Like, we don't want to do auto renewal.
We don't want to do this much of liability and things like that.
So there was a little bit of back and forth on that.
So I had to do a little bit of convincing internally as well as on the customer side.
And went through procurement.
And then I had to answer a short security questionnaire as well.
Because of AI.
They want
I to add an addendum on our terms of service with respect to AI and a few other security questions that I answered and got it through.
After that, what else?
Yeah, I think, yeah, it was reviewed by their legal and our legal, and then I forwarded that over to the signing authorities and got it over the line.
And it happened down to the wire on the last day of the quarter.
Like, it had to happen because I had committed that deal.
So if it had slipped, that would have been very bad on me.
Yeah, totally understand.
Crunch time.
Who was the client here, if you can share?
If you can't, that's okay.
Of course.
It's the Motley Fool.
Yeah, the Motley Fool, for sure.
How long did that expansion take?
How long was the total deal cycle?
How long did I spend?
I think it took about two and a half months.
Yeah, slightly less than three months.
Pretty quick.
Especially with all the different steps in the process that you had to go through.
Two questions there.
What was your biggest personal accomplishment?
And then what would you have done differently in that example?
Yeah, my personal accomplishment was that I had already done quite some groundwork on researching which are the various teams in Motley Fool, where are they based?
Like who could potentially benefit from Sanity and who could I go to with this already existing success story with Sanity, Motley Fool that is using Sanity already.
So I had done that groundwork fairly in advance.
I think that really helped me in kind of like fast tracking this and getting this over the line.
So that was good.
Nice.
And then what would you have done differently in hindsight?
Like where do you feel like maybe you went the wrong path or the question is like, well.
What was your biggest failure?
But I think that's a weird way to ask the question because you didn't have a failure.
You won the deal.
I won the deal.
Yeah, exactly.
What else?
I mean, yeah.
What would you have done differently?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I think in the process, I could have also tried to discover more use cases, which I did not do.
Like I was very thrilled that I got this expansion and I kind of like stopped there.
But I know Motley Fool has more potential because they're a big organization and they have a lot of revenue and stuff.
They're distributed.
